title: Network & Cloud Systems – Unified Answered Question Bank
description: Unified JSON for the GitHub Pages quiz app. Contains the original exam.html questions and all questions parsed from pastexams (2).pdf. PDF items include section metadata, answer keys, confidence/review flags, and source references.

schema.question.id: Stable unique ID. Use this for bookmarks.
schema.question.subjectId: Stable subject slug used for filtering.
schema.question.options: Original option order. The UI may shuffle options at render time.
schema.question.answerIndex: Index of the correct answer in the original options array.
schema.question.answer: Correct answer text. Useful if options are shuffled.
schema.question.explanation: Optional explanation shown after a wrong answer or after submission.
schema.question.sectionId: Stable section slug. Use this for subject/mastery filtering.
schema.question.reviewNeeded: true when OCR/figure/official-key review is needed.
schema.question.answerSource: Where the answer came from: exam.html key, PDF answer string, or concept inference.
schema.question.page: PDF page or page range when source is the PDF extracted text.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: According to NIST, cloud computing provides:
options: On-demand network access to shared configurable resources.
Dedicated hardware for every tenant.
Offline execution without networks.
Manual provisioning only.
questions.answer: On-demand network access to shared configurable resources.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: A key characteristic of cloud computing is:
options: On-demand self-service.
Fixed capacity per customer.
No virtualization.
No resource pooling.
questions.answer: On-demand self-service.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Resource pooling means:
options: Resources are shared among multiple tenants.
Each user owns the hardware.
Only one VM per server is allowed.
No monitoring is needed.
questions.answer: Resources are shared among multiple tenants.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Broad network access implies:
options: Services are available over the network via standard mechanisms.
Only local console access is possible.
Access requires a dedicated serial line.
Only administrators can connect.
questions.answer: Services are available over the network via standard mechanisms.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Measured service in cloud environments means:
options: Usage is monitored and can be billed.
Resources are unlimited and free.
Billing is always a fixed flat rate.
No metrics are collected.
questions.answer: Usage is monitored and can be billed.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Elasticity refers to:
options: Dynamic scaling up/down with demand.
Replacing servers once per year.
Only adding bigger CPUs.
Static allocation of VMs.
questions.answer: Dynamic scaling up/down with demand.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Pay-per-use is beneficial because:
options: Costs follow actual consumption.
It guarantees zero downtime.
It removes the need for capacity planning.
It disables multi-tenancy.
questions.answer: Costs follow actual consumption.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: CapEx vs OpEx: cloud adoption typically shifts costs toward:
options: Operational expenditure (OpEx).
Capital expenditure (CapEx) only.
No cost model changes.
Purely hardware depreciation.
questions.answer: Operational expenditure (OpEx).

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Capacity planning primarily aims to:
options: Reduce mismatch between demand and available capacity.
Maximize over-provisioning permanently.
Eliminate all peaks forever.
Avoid measuring traffic.
questions.answer: Reduce mismatch between demand and available capacity.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: In a Lag strategy, capacity is added:
options: After demand exceeds current capacity.
Before demand increases.
At random times.
Only when hardware is replaced.
questions.answer: After demand exceeds current capacity.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: In a Lead strategy, capacity is added:
options: In anticipation of demand growth.
Only after service disruption.
Only when costs drop.
Only at end of fiscal year.
questions.answer: In anticipation of demand growth.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Match strategy in capacity planning tries to:
options: Add capacity in small increments to follow demand.
Always stay below demand.
Always stay far above demand.
Avoid monitoring demand.
questions.answer: Add capacity in small increments to follow demand.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Vertical scaling means:
options: Replacing a resource with a more powerful one.
Adding more identical instances.
Splitting a VM into many pods.
Reducing network hops.
questions.answer: Replacing a resource with a more powerful one.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Horizontal scaling means:
options: Adding/removing identical resources.
Upgrading one server CPU.
Replacing RAM with faster RAM only.
Disabling redundancy.
questions.answer: Adding/removing identical resources.

questions.subject: Cloud basics / NIST / economics
questions.question: Vertical scaling is less common in clouds mainly because:
options: It may require downtime for replacement.
It is always cheaper than horizontal scaling.
It needs no maintenance.
It cannot increase capacity.
questions.answer: It may require downtime for replacement.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: IaaS mainly offers:
options: Virtualized compute, storage, and networking.
Complete end-user applications.
Only a programming language.
Only DNS resolution.
questions.answer: Virtualized compute, storage, and networking.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: PaaS mainly offers:
options: A runtime platform for developing/deploying apps.
Bare-metal servers to install any OS.
Only office applications.
Only router configuration.
questions.answer: A runtime platform for developing/deploying apps.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: SaaS mainly offers:
options: Complete applications delivered over the network.
Virtual machines you manage.
Hypervisors to install.
Physical switches.
questions.answer: Complete applications delivered over the network.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: In IaaS, the consumer is typically responsible for:
options: Guest OS and applications.
Physical datacenter cooling.
Hypervisor internals only.
Internet backbone routing.
questions.answer: Guest OS and applications.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: In SaaS, the consumer typically manages:
options: Only application-level configuration.
The hypervisor and host OS.
Physical servers.
Core routing protocols.
questions.answer: Only application-level configuration.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: A private cloud is:
options: Dedicated to a single organization.
Always publicly accessible.
Always free to use.
Only offered by governments.
questions.answer: Dedicated to a single organization.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: A public cloud is:
options: Available to the general public/organizations.
Restricted to one company only.
Always on-premises.
Unmetered by definition.
questions.answer: Available to the general public/organizations.

questions.subject: Service models / responsibility
questions.question: A hybrid cloud combines:
options: Private and public cloud resources.
Two different VLANs.
IPv4 and IPv6 only.
Two physical routers only.
questions.answer: Private and public cloud resources.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: Virtualization primarily provides:
options: An abstraction layer over hardware resources.
A new MAC protocol.
A new IP version.
Only containerization.
questions.answer: An abstraction layer over hardware resources.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: A guest OS is:
options: The OS running inside a VM.
The host OS on bare metal.
The hypervisor itself.
The network switch OS.
questions.answer: The OS running inside a VM.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: A host OS is:
options: The OS running on the physical machine (for type-2).
The OS inside a VM.
The SDN controller software only.
The container image.
questions.answer: The OS running on the physical machine (for type-2).

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: A type-1 hypervisor runs:
options: Directly on hardware.
Only inside containers.
On top of a host OS.
Only on laptops.
questions.answer: Directly on hardware.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: A type-2 hypervisor runs:
options: On top of a host OS.
Directly on hardware.
Only inside the BIOS.
Without any OS support.
questions.answer: On top of a host OS.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: Paravirtualization typically requires:
options: Guest OS modification/awareness.
No hypervisor.
Only physical servers.
Disabling drivers.
questions.answer: Guest OS modification/awareness.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: Hardware-assisted virtualization helps by:
options: Supporting privileged operations efficiently.
Removing the need for any OS.
Replacing IP routing.
Encrypting all traffic automatically.
questions.answer: Supporting privileged operations efficiently.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: One reason VMs can be heavier than containers is:
options: Each VM includes a full guest OS.
VMs cannot use virtual NICs.
VMs cannot be scheduled.
VMs share the kernel.
questions.answer: Each VM includes a full guest OS.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: VM migration is often used to:
options: Move a VM to another host for maintenance/load balancing.
Replace ARP with ND.
Tag Ethernet frames.
Create new VLANs.
questions.answer: Move a VM to another host for maintenance/load balancing.

questions.subject: Virtualization fundamentals
questions.question: Over-allocation in virtualization means:
options: Assigning more virtual resources than physically available.
Assigning exactly equal resources.
Disabling scheduling.
Making all VMs identical.
questions.answer: Assigning more virtual resources than physically available.

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: Containers differ from VMs because containers:
options: Share the host kernel.
Emulate hardware devices.
Require a guest OS per instance.
Disable process isolation.
questions.answer: Share the host kernel.

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: Linux namespaces primarily provide:
options: Isolation of system resources (PID, NET, MNT, etc.).
CPU accounting only.
Disk encryption.
BGP peering.
questions.answer: Isolation of system resources (PID, NET, MNT, etc.).

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: Linux cgroups primarily provide:
options: Resource limiting/accounting for groups of processes.
Filesystem abstraction per container.
VLAN tagging.
ARP processing.
questions.answer: Resource limiting/accounting for groups of processes.

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: A container image is best described as:
options: A packaged filesystem + metadata used to start containers.
A running container instance.
A physical disk drive.
A hypervisor module.
questions.answer: A packaged filesystem + metadata used to start containers.

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: A container is best described as:
options: A running instance created from an image.
A static template stored in a registry.
A BIOS replacement.
A routing protocol.
questions.answer: A running instance created from an image.

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: Containers often start faster than VMs because:
options: They avoid booting a full guest OS.
They require BIOS initialization.
They must run a hypervisor first.
They must rebuild the kernel.
questions.answer: They avoid booting a full guest OS.

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: Why might a Docker container fail to open a shell?
options: Minimal images may not include shell executables.
Docker forbids interactive commands.
The kernel blocks all processes.
Containers cannot run binaries.
questions.answer: Minimal images may not include shell executables.

questions.subject: Containers / Linux isolation
questions.question: A common container security concern is:
options: Shared kernel increases impact of kernel vulnerabilities.
Containers cannot isolate processes.
Containers always expose root access.
Containers must run on type-1 hypervisors.
questions.answer: Shared kernel increases impact of kernel vulnerabilities.

questions.subject: Docker specifics (conceptual)
questions.question: A Docker registry is used to:
options: Store and distribute container images.
Schedule pods across nodes.
Assign IP addresses.
Run BGP sessions.
questions.answer: Store and distribute container images.

questions.subject: Docker specifics (conceptual)
questions.question: In Docker, an image layer model mainly supports:
options: Efficient reuse and smaller downloads.
Mandatory encryption.
Routing optimization.
Collision detection.
questions.answer: Efficient reuse and smaller downloads.

questions.subject: Docker specifics (conceptual)
questions.question: A key goal of containerization is:
options: Application portability across environments.
Replacing TCP.
Eliminating virtualization.
Removing automation.
questions.answer: Application portability across environments.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: In Kubernetes, the smallest deployable unit is:
options: A Pod.
A Node.
A Registry.
A Hypervisor.
questions.answer: A Pod.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: A Kubernetes Pod typically contains:
options: One or more tightly coupled containers.
Only virtual machines.
Only storage volumes.
Only network routers.
questions.answer: One or more tightly coupled containers.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: A Kubernetes worker node typically:
options: Runs the container runtime to execute pods.
Stores only cluster policies.
Cannot be physical hardware.
Runs only the API server.
questions.answer: Runs the container runtime to execute pods.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: The Kubernetes control plane is responsible for:
options: Cluster state management and scheduling.
Forwarding every data packet.
Executing application containers directly.
Replacing DNS.
questions.answer: Cluster state management and scheduling.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: A Kubernetes Deployment is used to:
options: Manage desired state for replicated pods.
Assign MAC addresses.
Create VLAN tags.
Encrypt storage automatically.
questions.answer: Manage desired state for replicated pods.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: A Kubernetes Service is mainly used to:
options: Provide stable networking to reach pods.
Create container images.
Limit CPU usage of the host.
Install hypervisors.
questions.answer: Provide stable networking to reach pods.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: A key benefit of orchestration is:
options: Automated scaling and self-healing.
Eliminating networking needs.
Removing monitoring.
Guaranteeing zero failures.
questions.answer: Automated scaling and self-healing.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: A common reason to use multiple replicas in Kubernetes is:
options: High availability and load distribution.
To disable scheduling.
To avoid container images.
To remove service discovery.
questions.answer: High availability and load distribution.

questions.subject: Kubernetes basics
questions.question: Kubernetes scheduling decides:
options: Which node should run a pod.
Which VLAN should be used.
Which IP version must be used.
Which router learns MAC addresses.
questions.answer: Which node should run a pod.

questions.subject: SDN / OpenFlow / control-data plane
questions.question: Software Defined Networking (SDN) separates:
options: Control plane from data plane.
MAC from IP.
CPU from RAM.
VLAN from LAN.
questions.answer: Control plane from data plane.

questions.subject: SDN / OpenFlow / control-data plane
questions.question: The SDN controller typically:
options: Maintains a (logically) centralized view and programs devices.
Forwards packets at line rate.
Replaces all switches physically.
Is a Layer-2 hub.
questions.answer: Maintains a (logically) centralized view and programs devices.

questions.subject: SDN / OpenFlow / control-data plane
questions.question: An advantage of SDN is:
options: Network programmability and flexibility.
More collisions in Ethernet.
Removing routing tables.
Disabling policies.
questions.answer: Network programmability and flexibility.

questions.subject: SDN / OpenFlow / control-data plane
questions.question: OpenFlow is used to:
options: Install forwarding rules in switches.
Encrypt application payloads.
Assign IP addresses via DHCP.
Carry BGP updates.
questions.answer: Install forwarding rules in switches.

questions.subject: SDN / OpenFlow / control-data plane
questions.question: A scalability issue of centralized control can be:
options: Controller overload or bottlenecks.
ARP cannot work.
Ethernet cannot use full-duplex.
IPv6 cannot be used.
questions.answer: Controller overload or bottlenecks.

questions.subject: SDN / OpenFlow / control-data plane
questions.question: In SDN, the data plane is responsible for:
options: Forwarding packets based on rules.
Computing global routing policy.
Managing user accounts.
Creating container images.
questions.answer: Forwarding packets based on rules.

questions.subject: NFV concepts
questions.question: Network Function Virtualization (NFV) aims to:
options: Run network functions as software on standard hardware.
Replace IP addressing.
Remove virtualization.
Use only dedicated appliances.
questions.answer: Run network functions as software on standard hardware.

questions.subject: NFV concepts
questions.question: A VNF (Virtual Network Function) is:
options: A software implementation of a network function.
A VLAN identifier.
A physical Ethernet repeater.
A MAC address table.
questions.answer: A software implementation of a network function.

questions.subject: NFV concepts
questions.question: NFV orchestration is mainly about:
options: Lifecycle management of VNFs (deploy, scale, heal).
Tagging frames with VLAN IDs.
Running BGP updates.
Allocating IPv6 prefixes globally.
questions.answer: Lifecycle management of VNFs (deploy, scale, heal).

questions.subject: NFV concepts
questions.question: A key NFV benefit is:
options: Faster service deployment and flexibility.
Guaranteed zero latency.
Elimination of failures.
No need for servers.
questions.answer: Faster service deployment and flexibility.

questions.subject: Reliability / availability
questions.question: High availability is typically achieved by:
options: Redundancy and automation.
One extremely large server only.
Manual failover only.
Disabling monitoring.
questions.answer: Redundancy and automation.

questions.subject: Reliability / availability
questions.question: Fault tolerance usually requires:
options: Continuing operation despite failures.
Eliminating all redundancy.
Only vertical scaling.
No automation.
questions.answer: Continuing operation despite failures.

questions.subject: Reliability / availability
questions.question: A common approach for resilience is:
options: Replication across multiple instances.
Single points of failure.
Disabling health checks.
Avoiding load balancers always.
questions.answer: Replication across multiple instances.

questions.subject: Addressing / tunneling (no VPN/MPLS/wireless)
questions.question: A dual-stack host:
options: Runs IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously.
Uses NAT64 internally only.
Disables IPv4 completely.
Uses only IPv6 for routing.
questions.answer: Runs IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously.

questions.subject: Addressing / tunneling (no VPN/MPLS/wireless)
questions.question: IPv4-to-IPv6 tunneling techniques encapsulate:
options: IPv4 packets inside IPv6 packets.
Ethernet frames inside TCP.
UDP inside ICMP.
IPv6 inside Ethernet only.
questions.answer: IPv4 packets inside IPv6 packets.

questions.subject: Addressing / tunneling (no VPN/MPLS/wireless)
questions.question: A device with NAT64 functionality can:
options: Translate between IPv6 and IPv4 headers/addresses.
Operate on 64-bit IP addresses only.
Convert IPv6 packets directly into Ethernet frames.
Replace only the IPv6 destination address.
questions.answer: Translate between IPv6 and IPv4 headers/addresses.

questions.subject: Quality of service (DiffServ)
questions.question: In DiffServ, packet classification is usually done at:
options: Network edges.
Core routers only.
End hosts only.
Layer 2 switches only.
questions.answer: Network edges.

questions.subject: Quality of service (DiffServ)
questions.question: The DSCP field is located in:
options: The IP header.
The TCP header.
The Ethernet header.
The application payload.
questions.answer: The IP header.

questions.subject: Quality of service (DiffServ)
questions.question: Traffic shaping differs from policing because shaping:
options: Delays packets instead of dropping them immediately.
Drops packets immediately.
Encrypts packets.
Removes headers.
questions.answer: Delays packets instead of dropping them immediately.

questions.subject: Quality of service (DiffServ)
questions.question: A token bucket allows short bursts because:
options: Tokens can accumulate over time.
Packets are fragmented.
Routers buffer indefinitely.
Scheduling is always FIFO.
questions.answer: Tokens can accumulate over time.

questions.subject: Simple L2/L3 fundamentals (no wireless/MPLS/VPN)
questions.question: A VLAN-unaware switch:
options: Forwards frames without considering VLAN tags.
Routes between VLANs.
Requires trunk ports.
Drops all untagged frames.
questions.answer: Forwards frames without considering VLAN tags.

questions.subject: Simple L2/L3 fundamentals (no wireless/MPLS/VPN)
questions.question: IEEE 802.1Q tagging is used to:
options: Identify the VLAN of an Ethernet frame.
Encrypt Ethernet traffic.
Assign IP addresses.
Detect routing loops.
questions.answer: Identify the VLAN of an Ethernet frame.

questions.subject: Simple L2/L3 fundamentals (no wireless/MPLS/VPN)
questions.question: A hub differs from a repeater mainly because a hub:
options: Connects multiple devices.
Operates at layer 3.
Filters frames.
Uses VLAN tagging.
questions.answer: Connects multiple devices.

questions.subject: Simple L2/L3 fundamentals (no wireless/MPLS/VPN)
questions.question: A collision domain is reduced by using:
options: Switches instead of hubs.
Broadcast traffic.
Half-duplex links.
Only VLAN tagging.
questions.answer: Switches instead of hubs.

questions.subject: Simple L2/L3 fundamentals (no wireless/MPLS/VPN)
questions.question: In full-duplex Ethernet:
options: Collisions do not occur.
CSMA/CD is mandatory.
Broadcast is disabled.
MAC learning stops.
questions.answer: Collisions do not occur.

questions.subject: IPv6 neighbor discovery / multicast basics
questions.question: The Neighbor Discovery procedure in IPv6 is based on:
options: Multicast ICMPv6 messages.
Broadcast ICMPv6 messages.
ARPv6.
IPv4-only support.
questions.answer: Multicast ICMPv6 messages.

questions.subject: IPv6 neighbor discovery / multicast basics
questions.question: IPv6 Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) is performed using:
options: ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation.
ARP messages.
ICMPv6 Router Advertisement only.
DHCPv6 requests only.
questions.answer: ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation.

questions.subject: IPv6 neighbor discovery / multicast basics
questions.question: The solicited-node multicast address in IPv6 is used to:
options: Replace broadcast for address resolution.
Carry routing updates.
Advertise network prefixes.
Identify anycast services.
questions.answer: Replace broadcast for address resolution.

questions.subject: IPv6 neighbor discovery / multicast basics
questions.question: An IPv6 link-local address:
options: Cannot be routed beyond the local link.
Can be advertised through BGP globally.
Is deprecated.
Is always globally routable.
questions.answer: Cannot be routed beyond the local link.

questions.subject: BGP / routing basics (light)
questions.question: BGP is used for:
options: Exchange of routing information between autonomous systems.
Discovering hosts by MAC address.
Assigning VLAN IDs.
Collision detection in Ethernet.
questions.answer: Exchange of routing information between autonomous systems.

questions.subject: BGP / routing basics (light)
questions.question: The AS-PATH attribute in BGP is mainly used to:
options: Prevent routing loops.
Measure link cost.
Select the shortest physical cable.
Identify VLAN boundaries.
questions.answer: Prevent routing loops.

questions.subject: BGP / routing basics (light)
questions.question: Distance-vector routing protocols periodically send:
options: Their routing information to neighbors.
Only MAC tables to hosts.
Only link-state advertisements.
Only ARP caches to routers.
questions.answer: Their routing information to neighbors.

questions.subject: BGP / routing basics (light)
questions.question: A hierarchical routing structure helps to:
options: Improve scalability.
Eliminate routing tables entirely.
Avoid IP addressing.
Remove the need for metrics.
questions.answer: Improve scalability.

questions.subject: BGP / routing basics (light)
questions.question: In CIDR notation, the prefix length represents:
options: The number of bits identifying the network.
The hop count.
The number of packets per second.
The VLAN ID size.
questions.answer: The number of bits identifying the network.

questions.subject: BGP / routing basics (light)
questions.question: A /31 IPv4 subnet is typically used for:
options: Point-to-point links.
Broadcast LANs.
Public web servers only.
Large private LANs.
questions.answer: Point-to-point links.

questions.subject: BGP / routing basics (light)
questions.question: An addressing plan supporting aggregation should:
options: Assign contiguous address blocks.
Use random subnet allocation.
Avoid hierarchy.
Use only /32 routes.
questions.answer: Assign contiguous address blocks.

questions.subject: ICMPv6 / flow label
questions.question: An ICMPv6 error message is typically generated when:
options: A packet cannot be delivered.
A VLAN tag is missing.
A MAC address is unknown at L2.
A switch port is down only.
questions.answer: A packet cannot be delivered.

questions.subject: ICMPv6 / flow label
questions.question: The IPv6 Flow Label is intended to:
options: Identify packets belonging to the same flow.
Replace TCP/UDP ports.
Encrypt traffic.
Detect routing loops.
questions.answer: Identify packets belonging to the same flow.

questions.subject: NAT removal advantage
questions.question: The removal of NAT is often cited as an IPv6 advantage because:
options: End-to-end connectivity is restored.
Routing tables are always smaller.
Multicast is disabled.
QoS becomes automatic.
questions.answer: End-to-end connectivity is restored.

questions.question: The networks 192.168.1.0/24 and 192.168.2.0/24 can be aggregated into: cr45)
options: They cannot be aggregated.
questions.answer: They cannot be aggregated.

questions.question: the smallest aggregation that can contain the two IP networks 130.192.0.0/25 and 130.192.1.0/25 (2158)
questions.explanation: Correct answer assigned from the concept tested by the question. Verify against the lecturer's official key if one becomes available.

questions.subject: IPv4
questions.question: 1. Two stations A and B belong to the same physical network and have IP addresses 130.192.1.1/25 and 130.192.1.129/24 respectively (2004)
options: A communicates directly with B and vice versa
A communicates directly with B but not vice versa
A communicates with B only through a router
A cannot communicate with B
questions.answer: A communicates with B only through a router
questions.explanation: Correct answer assigned from the concept tested by the question. Verify against the lecturer's official key if one becomes available.

questions.subject: IPv4
questions.question: A Host A has address 192.168.1.1/24 and Host B 192.168.1.129/25. (26a)
